conditionEducation.name: PCOS (polycystic ovary syndrome)
conditionEducation.summary: A heterogeneous endocrine–metabolic disorder involving ovulatory dysfunction, androgen excess signs, and/or polycystic ovarian morphology on imaging—not “just bad periods.”
commonSymptoms: Acne, excess hair growth, or hair thinning
Elevated androgens or AMH on labs (clinician-ordered)
conditionEducation.mechanisms: Hyperandrogenemia, insulin resistance, and disrupted GnRH/LH signaling in ovarian theca and granulosa cells (supported by scRNA-seq studies in this project’s supplementary data).
conditionEducation.whenToSeekCare: Symptoms persisting ≥6–12 months, fertility goals, metabolic risk, or when symptoms are dismissed as “normal stress/hormones” without evaluation.

conditionEducation.name: Endometriosis
conditionEducation.summary: Estrogen-dependent tissue similar to the uterine lining growing outside the uterus, often causing inflammatory pelvic pain.
commonSymptoms: Cyclical pelvic pain (often worse with menses)
Pain with bowel movements or urination during menses
Heavy or irregular bleeding (overlap with PCOS possible)
conditionEducation.mechanisms: Chronic inflammatory microenvironment (e.g., CXCL12, IL6), progesterone resistance in lesions; eutopic endometrium may also show inflammatory signatures.
conditionEducation.whenToSeekCare: Pain limiting daily life, progressive worsening, or symptoms not responding to first-line therapies—referral to gynecology for imaging/laparoscopy discussion.

conditionEducation.name: Thyroid / prolactin disorders
conditionEducation.summary: Can mimic irregular cycles and metabolic symptoms; often ruled out with targeted labs.
conditionEducation.mechanisms: Thyroid hormones and prolactin affect GnRH pulsatility and ovulation.
conditionEducation.whenToSeekCare: Persistent cycle changes with systemic symptoms—ask about TSH and prolactin.

conditionEducation.name: Non-PCOS hyperandrogenism
conditionEducation.summary: Adrenal or other androgen sources can overlap with acne/hirsutism without PCOS.
conditionEducation.whenToSeekCare: Rapid virilization or atypical lab pattern.

symptomDifferentiationBase.pcos: May occur but is less often the primary hallmark; pain is not always cyclical.
symptomDifferentiationBase.endo: Often cyclical, may worsen with menses; bowel/bladder pain during periods is a classic clue.

symptomDifferentiationBase.pcos: Irregular ovulation / long cycles common; anovulatory bleeding patterns.
symptomDifferentiationBase.endo: Can be irregular or heavy; dysmenorrhea prominent.

symptomDifferentiationBase.pcos: Androgen-excess pattern (acne, hirsutism) is a core feature in many phenotypes.
symptomDifferentiationBase.endo: Less specific; not the primary diagnostic anchor.
Structural/inflammatory factors; strong association in supplementary endometriosis cohort.
symptomDifferentiationBase.other: Male factor, tubal disease, age-related decline.
